rationale_for_baseline_tier: Bilious vomiting, obstipation (~2 weeks), progressive abdominal distension and severe cramping, with tachycardia and inability to keep fluids down, constitute a possible large-bowel obstruction. The underlying cause is most likely faecal impaction, but a true mechanical/surgical obstruction, ischaemia or perforation cannot be excluded without abdominal examination, digital rectal examination and imaging — none available via telehealth. The patient is ambulant and not shocked (no peritonism reported on the source exam), so this is urgent ED assessment within hours (T4) rather than an immediate 000 ambulance (T5); however the threshold to T5 is low and clearly stated. Over-triaging every chronic-constipation flare to ED would be wrong, but this is an acute decompensation with an obstruction red-flag cluster, so T4 is the honest tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe, sharp/'razor-sharp' abdominal pain or a rigid, board-like abdomen
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals perforation/peritonitis — a surgical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly becomes severe and sharp, or the belly goes hard and rigid, call 000 immediately — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Constant unrelenting vomiting, unable to keep any fluid down for more than ~4 hours, or feeling faint/collapsing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: if vomiting is constant or no fluids tolerated for >4 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Marks worsening obstruction and dangerous dehydration needing emergency care and IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down for more than about 4 hours, the vomiting won't stop, or you feel faint, call 000 or get to an emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with a distended, tender or rigid abdomen
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests toxic megacolon or peritonitis — life-threatening and needs emergency management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever along with a swollen, rigid or very tender belly, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Passing fresh bright-red blood from the rectum
escalation_edges.rationale: Rectal bleeding needs urgent in-person assessment to exclude a serious cause.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you pass bright red blood from your bottom, go to the emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement — still not passing stool or flatus, continued distension, at the planned review
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: if unresolved at the 12–24 hour review
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure of conservative measures to relieve the obstruction requires escalation to in-person/hospital care for manual evacuation or admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you still haven't passed stool or wind and the bloating hasn't settled by the review, you need to be seen in hospital.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise immediate codeine cessation as part of the plan; the obstruction will not reliably resolve while opioids continue.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A primary caregiver is present and made the contact
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the safety-net advice and escalation triggers to the caregiver as well as the patient, and confirm they can arrange urgent transport to assessment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person urgently today — go to an emergency department (or a same-day face-to-face GP who can examine you and arrange an X-ray). Vomiting bile with a bloated belly and not passing stool can mean a bowel blockage that must be checked with an examination and imaging, which can't be done over telehealth. In the meantime: stop the codeine, take only small sips of clear fluid, and do NOT take any anti-diarrhoea medicine for the leaking stool.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend an emergency department today, or a same-day in-person GP; call 000 if any red-flag symptom occurs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Emergency departments and 000 are available 24 hours — do not wait for clinic hours if you have severe or sharp pain, constant vomiting, a rigid belly, fever or rectal bleeding.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a list of current medications (including the codeine) and describe the bilious vomiting, the ~2-week obstipation and the overflow soiling to the assessing clinician.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as routine chronic constipation and giving home laxative advice, missing the possible bowel obstruction.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised mechanical obstruction progresses to ischaemia or perforation, or an anti-diarrhoeal deepens the impaction — potentially fatal.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day urgent care without ensuring hands-on abdominal examination and imaging can occur may under-assess a possible obstruction.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a stable, ambulant patient with no peritonism is over-triage, though far safer than under-triage given the red flags.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is a benign faecal impaction or a true mechanical/surgical large-bowel obstruction — indistinguishable without examination and imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the obstruction red-flag cluster as unexcluded and arrange urgent in-person assessment; give conservative-management advice only as the plan once obstruction is excluded.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming it is 'just constipation', managing remotely with laxatives, or giving an anti-diarrhoeal for the overflow.
